title: Price Elasticity of Supply and its Determinants
description: Practice wha you've learned about price elasticity of supply and the determinants of price elasticity of supply in this exercise.
topic_path: Price Elasticity of Supply and its Determinants

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following formulas should be used to calculate price elasticity of supply?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What measure is used to determine how responsive a producer is to price changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What information is given by the price elasticity of supply of popcorn?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: How responsive sellers of popcorn are to changes in the price of popcorn
How responsive buyers of popcorn are to changes in the price of popcorn
How responsive popcorn buyers are to changes in the price of popcorn seasonings
How responsive popcorn buyers are to changes in income
How responsive popcorn sellers are to changes in the price of unpopped corn kernels

assessment_items.question_content:
When the price of socks was $\$4$ per pair, Theo’s Sock-Stravaganza supplied $10{,}000$ pairs of socks. When the price of socks was $\$5$ per pair, it supplied $17{,}500$ pairs of socks.

**What is the price elasticity of supply for socks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The price elasticity of supply is calculated using this formula:

$\begin{align}E_P^S &= \dfrac{\% \text{ change in }Q_S}{\% \text{ change in P}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{change in quantity supplied}}{\text{initial quantity supplied}}\times 100\%}{\dfrac{\text{change in price}}{\text{initial price}}\times 100\%}\end{align}$

The initial price is $\$4$, and $10{,}000$ pair of socks is supplied when the price is $\$4$. The new price is $\$5$ and $17{,}500$ pair of socks is supplied when the price is $\$5$. The formula using these values is:

$\begin{align}E_P^S &= \dfrac{\% \text{ change in }Q_S}{\% \text{ change in P}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{change in quantity supplied}}{\text{initial quantity supplied}}}{\dfrac{\text{change in price}}{\text{initial price}}}\times 100\%\end{align}$
There are $\$17{,}500$ pairs of socks supplied when the price is $\$5$, and $10{,}00$ pairs of socks is supplied when the price is $\$4$. The formula using these values is:

$\begin{align}E_P^S &= \dfrac{\% \text{ change in }Q_S}{\% \text{ change in P}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{change in quantity supplied}}{\text{initial quantity supplied}}}{\dfrac{\text{change in price}}{\text{initial price}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\dfrac{17,500 - 10,000}{10,000}}{\dfrac{\$5-\$4}{\$4}}\end{align}$
Price elasticity of supply equals 3:

$\begin{align}E_P^S &= \dfrac{\% \text{ change in }Q_S}{\% \text{ change in P}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{change in quantity supplied}}{\text{initial quantity supplied}}}{\dfrac{\text{change in price}}{\text{initial price}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\dfrac{17,500 - 10,000}{10,000}}{\dfrac{\$5-\$4}{\$4}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\dfrac{7,500}{10,000}}{\dfrac{\$1}{\$4}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{0.75}{0.25 }\\\\
&=3\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does price elasticity of supply measure?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An elasticity measures the *responsiveness* of one thing to a change in something else.
When you read the name of an elasticity, it tells you what is changing, and what it is changing in response to.
For example, you would read "the X elasticity of Y" as a measure of how much Y responds to a change in X.
For price elasticity of supply, Y is "supply" and "X" is price, so price elasticity of supply is a change in quantity supplied in response to a change in price.

choices.content: How much quantity supplied of a good changes in response to a change in that good’s price.
How much quantity supplied of a good changes in response to a change in the price of another good.
How much the supply curve shifts in response to a change in the price of a good.
How much the supply curve shifts in response to a change in the demand for a good.
How much the price of a good changes in response to a change in the quantity supplied.

assessment_items.question_content:
Quinn's Cupcake shop has a price elasticity of supply of $3.75$. 

**Which of the following statements is the correct interpretation of this price elasticity of supply?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: For every $\$3.75$ increase in price, Quinn will be willing to sell $1$ more cupcake
For every $\$3.75$ increase in price, Quinn will be willing to sell $1\%$ more cupcakes
For every $1\%$ increase in price,  Quinn will be willing to sell $3.75\%$ more cupcakes
For every $1\%$ increase in price, Quinn will be willing to sell $3.75$ more cupcakes
For every $3.75\%$ increase in price, Quinn is willing to sell $1$ more cupcake

assessment_items.question_content:
When the price of pickles increased $20\%$, the quantity supplied of pickles increased $80\%$. 

**What is the price elasticity of supply and how is that value interpreted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To calculate the price elasticity of supply, use this formula:

$E_P^S=\dfrac{\%\text{ change in }Q^S}{\%\text{ change in }P}$
Use the values of an $80\%$ change in quantity supplied in response to a $20\%$ change in price given:

$\begin{align}E_P^S&=\dfrac{\%\text{ change in }Q^S}{\%\text{ change in }P}\\\\
&=\dfrac{80\%}{20\%}
\end{align}$
The price elasticity of supply equals 4:

$\begin{align}E_P^S&=\dfrac{\%\text{ change in }Q^S}{\%\text{ change in }P}\\\\
&=\dfrac{80\%}{20\%}\\\\
&=4\end{align}$
Elasticities greater than 1 in magnitude are elastic, elasticities less than 1 in magnitude are inelastic, and elasticities equal to 1 are unit elastic.
A price elasticity of supply equal to 4 is elastic.

assessment_items.question_content:
The price of lemons increased $10\%$. 

**How much did the quantity of lemons increase if the price elasticity of supply of lemons is $2$, and how is that elasticity interpreted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Price elasticity of supply ($E_P^S$) is calculated using this formula:

$E_P^S=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}$

The price elasticity of supply is $2$ and the price of lemons increased $10\%$. We can plug these values into the formula:

$\begin{align} E_P^S&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}\\\\
2&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{10\%}\end{align}$

Next, solve for the percentage change in quantity supplied by multiplying both sides by $10\%$:

$\begin{align} E_P^S&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}\\\\
2&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{10\%} \\\\
\blue{10\%} \times 2&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{10\%} \times \blue{10\%} && \small\gray{\text{multiply both sides by 10\%}} \\\\
\blue{10\%} \times 2&=\% \Delta Q_s\\\\
20\%&=\% \Delta Q_s
\end{align}$
The percentage change in quantity supplied is $20\%$.
Price elasticity of supply greater than $1$ is elastic and smaller than $1$ is inelastic.
A price elasticity supply of $2$ is elastic.

assessment_items.question_content:
Farmers can easily grow raspberries or strawberries in the same soil using the same inputs and achieve the same output per acre in pounds.

**All else equal, what is the value of the relative price elasticity of supply of raspberries over two days or two years?**

[[☃ radio 4]]

choices.content: relatively inelastic in two days; relatively inelastic in two years
relatively elastic in two days; relatively inelastic in two years
relatively more elastic in two days than in 2 years
relatively inelastic in two days; relatively elastic in two years
relatively more inelastic in two years than in two days

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following would make price elasticity of supply more inelastic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Inputs to production are hard to obtain
Resources can easily be switched between the production of different goods
Resources are easily substitutable with each other

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following values of price elasticity of supply suggests that it might be difficult to acquire the inputs used to make the good?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If the resources used to produce goods are difficult to come by, sellers would not be able to easily to respond to an increase in price by supplying more of the good.
This means if the resources of production are hard to obtain, supply is likely to be inelastic.
An elasticity greater than $1$ is elastic.
An elasticity less than $1$ is inelastic.
A price elasticity of supply of 0.2 suggests that the resources used to produce this good might be difficult to obtain because it is inelastic.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following values of price elasticity of supply suggests that production uses inputs that are highly substitutable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If the resources used to produce goods are easy to substitute for each other, sellers would be able to easily to respond to an increase in price by supplying more of the good.
This means if the resources of production are easily substitutable, supply is likely to be elastic.
An elasticity less than $1$ is inelastic.
An elasticity greater than $1$ is elastic.
A price elasticity of supply of 20 suggests that the resources used to produce this good might be easily substitutable because it is very elastic.

assessment_items.question_content:
When the price of canned pickle juice increased $50\%$, the quantity supplied of pickle juice increased $40\%$. 

**What is the price elasticity of supply, and how is that value interpreted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To calculate the price elasticity of supply, use this formula:

$E_P^S=\dfrac{\%\text{ change in }Q^S}{\%\text{ change in }P}$
The price elasticity of supply equals 0.8:

$\begin{align}E_P^S&=\dfrac{\%\text{ change in }Q^S}{\%\text{ change in }P}\\\\
&=\dfrac{40\%}{50\%}\\\\
&=0.8\end{align}$
Elasticities greater than 1 in magnitude are elastic, elasticities less than 1 in magnitude are inelastic, and elasticities equal to 1 are unit elastic.
A price elasticity of supply equal to 0.8 is inelastic.

assessment_items.question_content:
**When the percentage change in price equals the percentage change in quantity supplied, how do we describe that elasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A proportionate response means that something changes in an identical way as some other thing. For example, a $10\%$ change in quantity supplied in response to a $10\%$ change in price.
A proportionate response results in a price elasticity of supply of 1.

If price elasticity of supply is greater than one, supply is elastic. If price elasticity of supply is less than one, supply is inelastic. If price elasticity of supply is equal to one, supply is unit elastic.

The answer:
Price elasticity of supply is unit elastic.

assessment_items.question_content:
In response to a change in price, the quantity supplied of sparkling lemonade increased by $10\%$. 

**If the price elasticity of supply is $0.5$, how much did price change, and how is this elasticity interpreted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Price elasticity of supply ($E_P^S$) is calculated using this formula:

$E_P^S=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}$

The price elasticity of supply is $0.5$ and the quantity of lemonade supplied increased by $10\%$. We can plug these values into the formula:

$\begin{align} E_P^S&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}\\\\
0.5&=\dfrac{10\%}{\%\Delta P}\end{align}$

Next, solve for the percentage change in price:

$\begin{align} E_P^S&=\dfrac{\% \Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}\\\\
0.5&=\dfrac{10\%}{\%\Delta P}\\\\
\blue{\%\Delta P} \times 0.5 &=\dfrac{10\%}{\%\Delta P} \times \blue{\%\Delta P} &&\small\gray{\text{ multiply both sides by the percentage change in price}}\\\\
\%\Delta P \times 0.5 &= 10\%\\\\
\dfrac{\% \Delta P \times 0.5}{\red{0.5}}&=\dfrac{10\%}{\red{0.5}}&&\small\gray{\text{ divide both sides by 0.5}}\\\\
\%\Delta P = 20\%
\end{align}$
The percentage change in price must have been $20\%$.
Price elasticity of supply greater than $1$ is elastic and smaller than $1$ is inelastic.
A price elasticity supply of $0.5$ is inelastic.
